gold_answer: It was caused by the identity-proxy caching the user's role/group assertions keyed by the stable SAML session_index, so right after the IdP group sync already-logged-in users could hit a stale cached role set and get RBAC 403s until they re-authenticated. We mitigated by doing targeted session revocations and temporarily lowering the session-index role cache TTL (e.g., 60s to 5s), and the long-term fix was to evict that cache on identity-change (group sync) webhooks so existing sessions immediately pick up new roles.
answer_facts: The intermittent RBAC 403s happened because identity-proxy cached users role and group assertions keyed by the stable SAML session_index.
Right after an IdP group membership sync, already logged in users could hit a stale cached role set and get RBAC 403s until they re-authenticated (logging out and back in).
Mitigation was to perform targeted session revocations and temporarily lower the session_index role cache TTL (for example from 60 seconds to 5 seconds).
The long-term server-side fix was to evict the role and group cache on identity-change (group sync) webhooks so existing sessions immediately pick up new roles.
The answer must not attribute the 403s to SAML assertion NotOnOrAfter/NotBefore validation failures caused by NTP drift at cache-expiry boundaries or to adding clock-skew tolerance/re-fetch fallback.
The answer must not involve JWKS/key-rotation issues such as a customer edge proxy stripping the 'kid' field, a malformed JWKS being cached, or forcing JWKS refreshes/adding JWKS integrity guards.

The answer must not claim the 403s were due to an Okta OIDC sidecar setting an incorrect cookie Domain (e.g., cluster.local), inconsistent group claim mapping across proxy replicas, or mitigation via sessionAffinity/scale-to-1.
The answer must not mention SAML AuthnInstant timezone parsing bugs, permissive datetime parsing flags, or scheduled export worker token-swap failures as the cause or fix.

question: In the March 2026 incident where long-lived Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams would reconnect after a brief upstream TCP reset and then show duplicated heartbeat frames while sometimes never emitting the terminal end-of-stream event, what was the root cause and what server-side fix resolved it?
gold_answer: Root cause was a race in the sse-gateway after a brief upstream TCP RST where the in-flight terminal end event flush interleaved with scheduled keepalive/heartbeat work, causing duplicated heartbeat frames and sometimes dropping the terminal end-of-stream event when the client resumed quickly. The fix was PR 2147 on the server to make terminal end emission atomic and strictly ordered ahead of heartbeats, and to delay/guard resume acceptance with a short grace window so a reconnect can't interleave with the final flush.
answer_facts: Root cause was a race in the sse-gateway after a brief upstream TCP RST where the in-flight terminal end event flush could interleave with scheduled keepalive or heartbeat work.
This race caused duplicated heartbeat frames after reconnect and could drop the terminal end-of-stream event when the client resumed quickly.
The server-side fix (PR 2147) made terminal end emission atomic and strictly ordered ahead of heartbeats.
The fix also delayed or guarded resume acceptance with a short grace window so a reconnect could not interleave with the final flush.

The answer must not attribute the incident to Last-Event-ID/resume headers being omitted or not forwarded across replicas, or to cross-node routing/KV cache warmup issues (that is a different unresolved issue).
The answer must not claim the fix was an edge/sidecar change to drain the TLS write queue before emitting FIN during keepalive retransmit (that is a different keepalive/FIN half-close problem).
The answer must not blame HTTP/2 multiplexing keepalive jitter or an nginx HTTP/2 frame coalescing canary causing RST_STREAM/GOAWAY-related truncation of terminal JSON metadata (separate incident).
The answer must not describe a WebSocket ping/pong control-frame race causing an abrupt WS 1006 close and missing EOF (wrong protocol and different root cause).
The answer must not cite TLS 1.3 0-RTT session-ticket replay by a customer firewall causing duplicated tokens (private deploy middlebox issue, not the March 2026 SSE heartbeat/end-event bug).
The answer must not conflate this with the March 13 NimbusHealth HTTP/2 SETTINGS/keepalive 5xx spike and runtime OOM crashes (different symptoms and remediation).

question: After the Console v4.3.2 rollout (March 2026), what caused saved dashboard filter presets to revert to the default right after you edit and save a linked sub-report in the widget editor modal, and what hotfix fixed it?
gold_answer: It was caused by a v4.3.2 frontend regression in the widget editor modal: the sub-report post-save handler (added in PR 4521 with propagateChanges enabled) dispatched a global presets:clear event that reset the dashboard's Redux filter preset state to Default. The hotfix (hotfix/disable-global-preset-clear) fixed it by scoping post-save events to the editor/local state so saving a linked sub-report no longer dispatches presets:clear to the global store.
answer_facts: The issue was caused by a Console v4.3.2 frontend regression in the widget editor modal.
After saving a linked sub-report, the sub-report post-save handler dispatched a global presets:clear event.
Dispatching presets:clear reset the dashboard Redux filter preset state back to Default, making saved filter presets appear to revert immediately.
The hotfix named hotfix/disable-global-preset-clear fixed the issue.
The hotfix fixed it by scoping the post-save behavior to the editor or local state so saving a linked sub-report no longer dispatched presets:clear to the global store.

The answer must not attribute the preset reset to an account plan upgrade or migration job dropping a saved_filters map (that issue involves plan changes and backend migration behavior, not editing a linked sub-report in the widget editor modal).
The answer must not describe pinned filters being cleared when opening dashboards via embedded/public share links (that is about share/iframe behavior, not editing and saving a sub-report).

The answer must not claim the cause was async filter propagation ordering differences between panels and an SLO card (that issue is about inconsistent counts and filter ordering, not presets reverting to default after a modal save).
The answer must not involve tracehook/project-scope leakage from saved-filter sharing (that is a security/scoping problem unrelated to dashboard presets resetting after sub-report edits).

question: During a March 2026 prod us-east-1 dedicated incident where a sudden burst of high-concurrency streaming traffic caused intermittent 502/504s due to proxy ephemeral port pressure, what mitigations were applied (replica changes and any kernel/sysctl tuning) and-per the ingress proxy connection-entropy playbook-what RCA artifacts should be captured while the issue is ongoing?
gold_answer: Mitigations applied were to scale up the proxy deployment replicas (with a rolling/sequential restart of some proxy pods to redistribute ephemeral-port usage) and to widen net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range on the impacted proxy nodes from the default 32768-60999 to 10240-65535 via a DaemonSet. While the issue is ongoing, capture RCA artifacts per the connection-entropy playbook: increase trace sampling to ~25% for the impacted route for the next ~2 hours, take a ~90-second tcpdump/pcap on an affected proxy and upload it, save the metrics window (30 minutes before, during, and ~2 hours after), and record the list of impacted tenants with exact error counts and timestamps.
answer_facts: Mitigation: scale up the ingress proxy deployment replicas to handle the burst in concurrency
Mitigation: perform a rolling or sequential restart of some proxy pods to redistribute ephemeral port usage
Mitigation: widen net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range on impacted proxy nodes from 32768-60999 to 10240-65535
Mitigation: apply the net.ipv4.ip_local_port_range change via a DaemonSet on the impacted proxy nodes
RCA artifact: increase trace sampling to about 25 percent for the impacted route for roughly the next 2 hours while the issue is ongoing
RCA artifact: capture a roughly 90 second tcpdump or pcap on an affected proxy and upload it
RCA artifact: save the relevant metrics window covering about 30 minutes before the incident, during the incident, and about 2 hours after
RCA artifact: record the list of impacted tenants with exact error counts and timestamps

The answer must not claim the incident mitigations included raising proxy file-descriptor ulimits, epoll/backlog tuning, or 'too many open files' fixes; those mitigations are from other March 2026 edge socket/FD incidents, not this ephemeral-port-pressure incident.

The answer must not claim sysctl changes were applied for net.ipv4.tcp_tw_reuse, tcp_fin_timeout, tcp_tw_limit, or tcp_max_syn_backlog as part of this incident's applied mitigations; the documented applied tuning here was widening ip_local_port_range.

The answer must not describe DNS failover/cross-region route dampening or GPU/NVML watchdog-driven runtime crash recovery; that is a different incident pattern than the us-east-1 proxy ephemeral port pressure burst described here.
The answer must not say this happened in us-west-2 or involved an embedding-sweep tail-latency degradation/connection leak; that is a separate customer issue in a different region.

The answer must not include VPC E2E test harness/synthetic probe procedures or artifacts as the RCA capture list; the requested artifacts come from the ingress proxy connection-entropy playbook (pcap, traces, metrics window, tenant/error list), not the synthetic probe system.

question: In the hosted API incident in late April 2026 where streaming requests started returning 502/504 and non-streaming requests also saw a sharp tail-latency increase, what was the underlying trigger (involving an edge keepalive/connection-TTL change) and what immediate mitigations were used to restore service?
gold_answer: It was triggered by an edge configuration change that cut upstream connection keepalive/TTL from 90s to 10s, increasing connection churn and exposing a streaming-proxy TCP write-stall bug that caused 502/504 on streaming and sharp tail-latency for non-streaming. Service was restored by rolling restarting the streaming-proxy fleet to clear stuck buffers, rolling back the edge TTL/keepalive setting to 90s, and applying temporary traffic shaping (soft concurrency limits and rejecting new sessions on overloaded proxies).
answer_facts: The incident was triggered by an edge configuration change that reduced upstream connection keepalive or connection TTL from 90 seconds to 10 seconds.
Reducing keepalive or TTL increased upstream connection churn.
The increased connection churn exposed a streaming-proxy TCP write-stall bug, leading to 502 and 504 errors on streaming requests and sharp tail-latency increases on non-streaming requests.
Service was restored by rolling restarting the streaming-proxy fleet to clear stuck buffers.
Service was restored by rolling back the edge keepalive or TTL setting from 10 seconds back to 90 seconds.
Service was restored by applying temporary traffic shaping such as soft concurrency limits and rejecting new sessions on overloaded proxies.
The answer must not describe the 2026-04-11 incident involving scheduler coalescing, acceptor backoff/hedge loop regressions, or forced KV-lease refresh as the root cause or mitigations.

The answer must not describe the 2026-04-18 incident where session-checkpoint serialization/compression and synchronous session-store writes caused stalls, nor list mitigations like disabling compression or switching session persistence to best-effort.
The answer must not describe the March 2026 us-east-1 streaming stall event mitigated by disabling NGINX ingress proxy buffering, changing chunk flush intervals, or per-client rate limiting.
The answer must not claim the mitigation was primarily manual scaling of model pools or enabling a fallback model variant for /v1/generate (those actions are from a separate watch digest).

question: During the us-east canary that normalized/rotated the session cookie domain for cross-subdomain routing, long-poll streaming clients started losing their pinned model version and getting routed to a fallback model. What was the root cause in the edge/session-manager flow, and what immediate mitigations were applied (platform-side and customer-side)?
gold_answer: Root cause: the edge canary rewrote/normalized the Set-Cookie domain (e.g., to .redwood.ai) and long-poll streaming clients kept sending the old host-scoped cookie; session-manager treated the cookie domain as mismatched/invalid, considered the session unauthenticated for pin reconciliation, dropped the pinned routing key, and the router then selected the fallback model. Immediate mitigations: platform rolled back/disabled the cookie-domain rewrite for the us-east canary cohort; customer was instructed to force a session refresh (POST /v1/sessions/refresh) to restore the pin for affected long-poll sessions.
answer_facts: The us-east edge canary rewrote or normalized the Set-Cookie Domain attribute to a cross-subdomain domain (for example, .redwood.ai).
Long-poll streaming clients continued sending an old host-scoped session cookie after the domain normalization.
In the edge to session-manager flow, session-manager treated the cookie as domain-mismatched or invalid and considered the session unauthenticated for pin reconciliation.
Because the session was treated as unauthenticated, session-manager dropped the pinned routing key (pinned model version).
With the pinned routing key dropped, the router selected a fallback model.
Platform-side mitigation was to roll back or disable the cookie-domain rewrite for the us-east canary cohort.
Customer-side mitigation was to force a session refresh via POST /v1/sessions/refresh to restore the pin for affected long-poll sessions.

The answer must not attribute the incident to an edge header rewrite dropping an affinity cookie when both legacy and new cookies with different path/domain attributes are present (BrightLearn scenario).
The answer must not claim the pin loss was due to a deferred pin-commit race with a concurrent canary weight update and an admin sweep/commit-coordinator gating fix (AcmePayments scenario).
The answer must not cite backpressure-induced control-plane commit delays or commit-worker CPU throttling during a QPS spike as the reason for fallback routing (NarrativeAI scenario).

The answer must not blame a dedicated pool migration, edge-cache stale TTL/refresh suppression, or an orchestrator pin reconciler batching delay/max_batch_delay setting for the fallback behavior (NimbleHealth scenario).
The answer must not mention preemptive backfill/standby node reassignment missing pin metadata or the customer workaround header 'x-redwood-disable-prewarm-backfill: true' (NovaChat scenario).

question: After the March 9, 2026 embedding-catalog promotion that made a 1024-d embedding model the default, a dedicated Hosted API tenant (us-west with eu-west failover) saw /v1/rerank start timing out with 512-vs-1024 vector length mismatches when using the alias open-embed-default. What was the confirmed root cause, and what short-term mitigation and permanent fix did we deploy?
gold_answer: Root cause: the March 9, 2026 catalog promotion/migration failed to persist tenant-specific alias overrides, so for that dedicated tenant the alias open-embed-default unexpectedly resolved to open-embed-1024-v2 (1024-d) instead of the legacy 512-d model, and the reranker's strict dimension check then hit an expensive error/retry path that drove timeouts. Short-term mitigation: apply a per-tenant routing override / explicit pin to open-embed-512-v1 (and restart long-running workers / rerun affected batches). Permanent fix: deploy the catalog alias-persistence hotfix and roll it out to restore correct per-tenant alias resolution (open-embed-default -> open-embed-512-v1) and prevent future alias drift.
answer_facts: The March 9, 2026 embedding catalog promotion migration failed to persist tenant-specific alias overrides for a dedicated Hosted API tenant.
Because the tenant-specific override was not persisted, the alias open-embed-default unexpectedly resolved to open-embed-1024-v2 (1024 dimensional) instead of the legacy 512 dimensional model.
The reranker performed a strict embedding dimension check, and the resulting 512 vs 1024 mismatch triggered an expensive error or retry path that caused /v1/rerank timeouts.
Short-term mitigation was to apply a per-tenant routing override (explicit pin) so open-embed-default used open-embed-512-v1 for that tenant.
Permanent fix was to deploy and roll out a catalog alias-persistence hotfix to restore correct per-tenant alias resolution (open-embed-default to open-embed-512-v1) and prevent future alias drift.

Do not attribute the incident to mixed ONNX-quantized vs FP32 embedding pipelines or 1536-d vs 1024-d vectors; that is a different customer issue and not the open-embed-default alias drift after the March 9 promotion.

Do not say the fix was adding a rerank request validator to reject mixed-dimension candidate lists within a single request or recommending zero-padding 512-d vectors; that describes a separate rerank validation incident unrelated to the catalog default promotion and alias open-embed-default.

Do not claim the root cause was malformed externally-computed embeddings (e.g., 384-d proto payloads with stray footer bytes) or GPU OOM spill/fallback determinism; that is a different case and not tied to open-embed-default resolving to the wrong model.

Do not present the schema-driven model contract/canary/deprecation playbook work as the deployed permanent fix for this incident; it is a broader planning/design effort, not the specific alias-persistence hotfix used here.

question: For Incident Bot v2, when responders use break-glass during a Sev0/Sev1 incident to view tenant-scoped config diffs or run privileged telemetry queries, which audit events must be emitted and how long are those audit events retained (SIEM vs immutable archive)? Also, what baseline vs break-glass permission scopes does the bot use for these reads?
gold_answer: When break-glass is used in a Sev0/Sev1 incident, the bot must emit audit events for the break-glass session (incident_bot.break_glass.activate and incident_bot.break_glass.deactivate when it expires/is revoked) and for each privileged read: incident_bot.config_diff.read for tenant-scoped config diff reads and incident_bot.telemetry.query for privileged tenant-scoped telemetry queries (with break_glass=true and a break_glass_session_id on calls made under break-glass). These audit events are retained 400 days in the searchable SIEM and 7 years in an immutable/WORM archive. For permissions, baseline reads use config.read:diff_redacted and telemetry.read:diagnostics_templates; break-glass uses config.read:diff_expanded_redacted for expanded redacted config diffs and telemetry.read:tenant_scoped for tenant-scoped telemetry templates.
answer_facts: During Sev0/Sev1 break-glass use, the bot must emit audit events for the break-glass session activation and deactivation (incident_bot.break_glass.activate and incident_bot.break_glass.deactivate).
During break-glass, the bot must emit an audit event for each tenant-scoped config diff read (incident_bot.config_diff.read).
During break-glass, the bot must emit an audit event for each privileged tenant-scoped telemetry query (incident_bot.telemetry.query).
Privileged read audit events emitted under break-glass must include break_glass=true and a break_glass_session_id.
These audit events are retained 400 days in the searchable SIEM.
These audit events are retained 7 years in an immutable/WORM archive.
Baseline (non-break-glass) config diff reads use the permission scope config.read:diff_redacted.
Baseline (non-break-glass) telemetry reads use the permission scope telemetry.read:diagnostics_templates.
Break-glass config diff reads use the permission scope config.read:diff_expanded_redacted.
Break-glass telemetry reads use the permission scope telemetry.read:tenant_scoped.
The answer must not claim break-glass is allowed for Sev2 or Sev3 incidents; it is restricted to Sev0/Sev1.

The answer must not say deploy metadata reads or SLO link generation require privileged audit events by default; the privileged audit requirement is for tenant-scoped config diffs and privileged telemetry.
The answer must not state SIEM retention is 30 days or 180 days; those are deploy-metadata data retention figures, not incident-bot privileged access audit retention.
The answer must not claim audit events are retained only in SIEM (or only in an archive); they must be in SIEM (400 days) and an immutable archive (7 years).
The answer must not replace the required break-glass lifecycle events with unrelated Slack UX events (e.g., only logging a slack action invoke) or omit break-glass activate/deactivate entirely.
The answer must not say the bot's baseline telemetry scope is tenant-scoped; tenant-scoped telemetry is a break-glass-only scope.

question: What was the root cause and planned server-side fix for the March 2026 incident where function-call webhook callbacks were retried after a timeout and the JSON payload stayed the same, but the HMAC signature header changed between the first delivery and the retry due to gzip/encoding differences?
gold_answer: Root cause: the gateway generated the HMAC signature over the post-transport encoded body (after gzip/compression), and the retry path re-encoded the same JSON with different gzip/encoding details (e.g., gzip envelope metadata/CRLF normalization), so the raw bytes changed and the signature header differed even though the JSON payload was unchanged. Planned server-side fix: move signing to the canonical logical payload before compression/encoding so all retries verify regardless of gzip/transport differences (and expose a canonicalization/version indicator header).
answer_facts: The gateway computed the HMAC signature over the post-transport encoded request body (after gzip or other compression/encoding).

On retry, the same JSON payload was re-encoded with different gzip/encoding details (for example gzip envelope metadata or CRLF normalization), changing the raw bytes and therefore changing the HMAC signature header even though the JSON was unchanged.

Planned server-side fix was to sign a canonical representation of the logical payload before any compression/encoding so retries verify regardless of gzip or transport differences, and to expose a canonicalization or version-indicator header.
Do not attribute this incident to idempotency or trace-id header normalization by an ALB/proxy (e.g., '+' vs space or base64 variants) causing duplicate executions; that is a different issue.
Do not claim the root cause was missing a stable tool invocation ID in the webhook payload/headers; that relates to deduplication guidance for retries, not signature mismatch due to gzip encoding.

Do not say the signature changed because of OAuth token refresh or because base64 vs base64url encoding/padding differences were used for the signature header; that is a separate SDK/auth-related incident.
Do not describe this as an API gateway saturation / retry storm / runtime OOM incident with elevated 5xx rates; that outage is unrelated to HMAC signatures changing between first delivery and retry.

Do not answer using the general webhook contract document as if it described the specific March 2026 gzip-related signature mismatch incident; it provides generic guidelines, not the incident's root cause and fix.

question: After rotating a Hosted API key in the Console, why might the Usage → Ad-hoc Query still show request IDs tied to the old key and 'View trace' links that 404, and what immediate mitigation did Support/SRE apply before a proper fix shipped?
gold_answer: Because the Console Usage/Ad-hoc Query was serving stale/legacy cached usage rows and trace metadata keyed to the old api_key_id (not properly invalidated on rotation, with some pods still reading the legacy cache prefix), and the usage/trace indexing hadn't been remapped yet so generated trace anchors still referenced the old key, leading to trace lookups that 404. Support/SRE mitigated immediately by flushing the org's legacy Redis cache prefix and enabling a temporary console bypass so orgs with a recent rotation read live from the index instead of cache.
answer_facts: Console Usage/Ad-hoc Query was showing stale cached usage rows and trace metadata keyed to the old api_key_id because cache entries were not properly invalidated on key rotation (with some services still reading the legacy cache prefix).
Usage/trace indexing had not yet been remapped after rotation, so generated trace links still referenced the old key and trace lookups returned 404.
Support/SRE mitigated immediately by flushing the orgs legacy Redis cache prefix and enabling a temporary console bypass so recently rotated orgs read live from the index instead of cache.

The answer must not attribute the 404 trace links to saved dashboard query timeouts, analytics-engine query planner full-scan behavior, OOM/GC issues, or missing/empty trace_id payloads for >24h ranges.

The answer must not claim the post-rotation problem was cross-region routing/residency-claim omission that caused p95 latency spikes, nor suggest using API rotation with a residency header as the mitigation.
The answer must not describe tenant concurrency exhaustion or 429 bursts during key handover, nor mitigations like backoff/jitter, reducing overlap windows, or increasing concurrency limits.

The answer must not mention duplicate webhook deliveries from SDK retry behavior during mid-request rotation in a private/VPC deployment, nor mitigations like webhook idempotency checks or SDK version pinning.

The answer must not explain the symptom as intermittent 401s due to customer not updating all services, propagation delays after revocation, pasting key IDs instead of key values, or environment variable mismatches.

question: In the March 2025 incident where eu-west-1 ran short on GPUs and the router temporarily failed eligible traffic over to us-east-1, what conditions/thresholds triggered enabling region fallback and which traffic was excluded? Also, per the region pinning policy, what kinds of request/response data are considered 'customer content' that must stay in-region for residency-strict traffic?
gold_answer: Region fallback (eu-west-1 -> us-east-1) was enabled when router triggers hit saturation > 90% or 503 rate > 0.5% (preceded by alerts like available_gpu_count < 5 and saturation > 95% with queue wait > 400ms). It only applied to eligible routes/tenants that allow cross-region; any traffic marked data_residency_strict (residency-strict/pinned) was excluded. For residency-strict traffic, "customer content" that must stay in-region includes prompts/messages/input text, tool/function-call arguments, uploaded documents and RAG passages, model outputs (including partial streaming tokens), embeddings input text and embedding vectors, rerank inputs and rerank outputs, fine-tuning/adapter payloads if routed via Hosted APIs, any debug payloads with raw request/response bodies, and any derived artifacts that could reconstruct content (e.g., token logs/decoded segments/sampled prompt fragments).
answer_facts: Region fallback from eu-west-1 to us-east-1 was enabled when router triggers hit saturation greater than 90 percent or a 503 error rate greater than 0.5 percent.
Region fallback only applied to eligible routes or tenants that allow cross-region routing.
Traffic marked data_residency_strict (residency-strict or pinned) was excluded from region fallback.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes prompts, messages, and other input text (including tool or function-call arguments).
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes uploaded documents and RAG passages.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes model outputs, including partial streaming tokens.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes embeddings input text and embedding vectors, plus rerank inputs and rerank outputs.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes fine-tuning or adapter payloads when routed via Hosted APIs.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes debug payloads containing raw request or response bodies.
For residency-strict traffic, customer content includes derived artifacts that could reconstruct content, such as token logs, decoded segments, or sampled prompt fragments.
The answer must not use the July 2025 Private VPC incident triggers (upstream timeout rate > 0.8% or node connectivity check failures) or its us-east-1 -> us-west-2 region pair.
The answer must not describe April 2025 model fallback behavior (fallback to a smaller model) or its canary/quality-regression details, since the query is about region fallback in March 2025.

The answer must not cite the June 2025 Dedicated-to-Hosted fallback triggers (503 rate > 1% or unhealthy_nodes >= 2) or claim the fallback was from a Dedicated pool to a Hosted pool as the March 2025 event.
The answer must not include the retrospective guardrail 'maximum fallback percentage per route (default 10%)' as the trigger for enabling fallback in the March 2025 incident.

The answer must not rely on the PRD's error-contract details (e.g., HTTP 409 Conflict, specific residency error codes) because the query asks for the policy's definition of customer content, not the public error format.
The answer must not use the Q1 2025 fallback review summary as the source of thresholds, since it lists events but does not define the March 2025 trigger thresholds or exclusions.

question: In a private deployment, we saw a security bug where an RBAC exclusion rule (meant to block admin/superuser access) allowed access to an admin route when a JWT omitted the entitlements claim in mid-March 2026. What was the root cause in the policy evaluator, and what were the immediate workaround and the server-side fix plus rollout timeline?
gold_answer: Root cause: the policy evaluator's not_in operator coerced an absent entitlements claim into a single empty-string value, so the membership checks all returned false and the overall exclusion expression evaluated true, granting access. Immediate workaround: ensure JWTs always include entitlements as an empty array (and/or add an explicit exists(user.entitlements) check and deny when it's missing). Server-side fix: update the evaluator to treat missing claims as an empty set for not_in and remove the absent->empty-string coercion; canary to staging on 2026-03-15 with rollout/patch bundle for private control-plane customers on 2026-03-18.
answer_facts: The policy evaluator not_in operator coerced a missing entitlements claim into a single empty-string value.
Because of the empty-string coercion, not_in membership checks returned false and the exclusion expression evaluated true, granting access.
An immediate workaround was to ensure JWTs always include entitlements as an empty array.
An immediate workaround was to add an explicit exists(user.entitlements) check and deny when entitlements is missing.
The server-side fix was to treat missing claims as an empty set for not_in and remove the absent-to-empty-string coercion.
The fix was canaried to staging on 2026-03-15.
The rollout or patch bundle for private control-plane customers was on 2026-03-18.

Do not attribute the incident to identity-proxy header trimming or max header size enforcement (that issue causes missing roles/tenant_bindings and 401/403s for service-account jobs, not an RBAC exclusion bypass).

Do not claim Azure AD Conditional Access/device compliance policy changes were the cause (that scenario yields 403 device_compliance_required and is driven by IdP policy/claim availability, not not_in evaluation).
Do not describe tenant-claim namespace stripping or tolerant tenant lookup as the root cause (that leads to cross-tenant 403 denies due to claim key rewriting, not unexpected admin-route allows).
Do not explain this as SCIM/entitlement propagation lag or indexer batching delays (those cause transient 403s after group updates, not a security bypass when a claim is omitted).
Do not blame gateway routing fallthrough/predicate mismatches (those are route-selection issues typically producing 401s, unrelated to RBAC not_in claim-missing behavior).
Do not reference route predicate serialization/rollout pinning incidents (those concern canary rollout mechanics and predicate encoding mismatches, not JWT claim handling in RBAC).

question: In the mid-March 2026 eu-west canary rollout where sticky fallback kept pinning requests back onto the failing canary instead of the stable quantized variant, what configuration mistake caused the loop and what mitigations were applied to stop it?
gold_answer: The loop was caused by the rollout fallback_policy using sticky fallback with preferred_variant set to the ambiguous label "latest", which resolved back to the canary (also tagged "latest") instead of the stable-quantized variant, and the long sticky_ttl kept sessions pinned. Mitigations: pause the canary rollout, change fallback to a forced_variant of stable-quantized, drain the eu-west canary pods, reduce sticky TTL, and enable a temporary eu-west circuit-breaker to force stable routing on elevated error rate.
answer_facts: The loop was caused by the fallback_policy using sticky fallback with preferred_variant set to the ambiguous label "latest".
The label "latest" resolved back to the canary because the canary was also tagged "latest", instead of resolving to the stable-quantized variant.
A long sticky_ttl kept sessions pinned to the canary and sustained the loop.
Mitigation included pausing the canary rollout.
Mitigation included changing fallback to a forced_variant of stable-quantized.
Mitigation included draining the eu-west canary pods.
Mitigation included reducing the sticky TTL.
Mitigation included enabling a temporary eu-west circuit-breaker to force stable routing when error rate was elevated.

The answer must not attribute the cause to a canary model-variant typo like "v2-8q not found" or to smart-router bouncing between us-west and eu-west due to low capacity; that is a different incident.

The answer must not describe a greedy routing rule like matching "region:eu*" or KV-cache incompatibility between 7b/13b quant variants; that routing-policy incident is separate from this eu-west canary rollout.

The answer must not claim the mitigations were "clear sticky pins for /generate" or applying the control-plane toggle "fallback_disable_sticky_during_canary=true"; those actions belong to a different canary incident.
The answer must not cite a missing hop-count header or edge-router re-ingest loop from a February 2026 dedicated-routing postmortem; that is unrelated to this mid-March eu-west canary rollout.

The answer must not say the event was a customer traffic surge that routed a pinned dedicated deployment to a legacy quantized variant (e.g., redwood/quant-13b-q4) and was mitigated via per-customer strict pinning; that is a different March 12 review.

question: For a shadow evaluation running in a private deployment using an evaluator sidecar, what fields are required in each shadow probe telemetry record, and which run-lifecycle telemetry events must the evaluator emit?
gold_answer: Each shadow probe telemetry record must include: probe_id, tenant_id, deployed_model, deployment_variant, quant_profile, hardware_tag, prompt_family, seed_context_hash, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_generated, top_level_metrics, error_code, timestamp_utc. The evaluator must emit these run-lifecycle telemetry events: eval.run.start, eval.run.progress, eval.run.complete.
answer_facts: Each shadow probe telemetry record must include probe_id, tenant_id, deployed_model, deployment_variant, quant_profile, hardware_tag, prompt_family, seed_context_hash, latency_ms, tokens_input, tokens_generated, top_level_metrics, error_code, and timestamp_utc.
The evaluator must emit the eval.run.start run-lifecycle telemetry event.
The evaluator must emit the eval.run.progress run-lifecycle telemetry event.
The evaluator must emit the eval.run.complete run-lifecycle telemetry event.
Do not include optional probe fields like raw_response_snippet, annotator_score, or kv_cache_hit as required fields.

Do not use the anchored shadow fleet per-request fields (e.g., request_id, origin_route, model_variant, tokens_in/tokens_out, scorer_metrics, exposure_tag) in place of the private shadow probe record fields.
Do not use the slice handoff/shadow fields (e.g., bundle_id, slice_id, token_count, perplexity_window, rsi, safety_flag) as the required fields for each private shadow probe telemetry record.
Do not list non-lifecycle telemetry such as backpressure decision events/metrics (e.g., eval.backpressure.* or telemetry/backpressure schema) as required run-lifecycle events.
Do not replace the required run-lifecycle events with artifact/index output requirements (e.g., index.json fields like started_at, completed_at, checksums, telemetry_digest).

question: After the org GPG signing-key rotation on 2026-03-09, our GitLab release promotion pipelines started failing on prod release runners during artifact verification. What was the root cause of the signature verification failures, and what immediate operational mitigations unblocked hotfix promotions while the permanent fix was rolled out?
gold_answer: Root cause: during the 2026-03-09 GPG key rotation, some prod release runners imported a malformed/truncated public key because the internal keysync service mishandled chunked-transfer responses from the internal keyserver, leaving runners with bad/outdated key material; combined with drift in GPG versions/keyrings across runner images this produced BAD_SIGNATURE/invalid packet errors during verification. Immediate mitigations: temporarily roll back the signing host to the previous signing key and, in parallel, unblock hotfix promotions by targeting a small set of prod runners with a forced keyring refresh/import of the correct public key (and temporarily allowing verification with the previous key on those runners) while the keysync fix and broader key rollout proceeded.
answer_facts: During the 2026-03-09 GPG key rotation, some prod release runners imported a malformed or truncated public key.
The malformed key was caused by the internal keysync service mishandling chunked-transfer responses from the internal keyserver.
Bad or outdated key material on runners, combined with drift in GPG versions and keyrings across runner images, produced BAD_SIGNATURE and invalid packet errors during artifact verification.
An immediate mitigation was to temporarily roll back the signing host to the previous signing key.

Hotfix promotions were unblocked by targeting a small set of prod runners with a forced keyring refresh to import the correct public key and temporarily allowing verification with the previous key on those runners while the permanent fix rolled out.

The answer must not attribute the failure to CI signing failing due to missing private/secret keys or pinentry/interactive GPG issues (those are signing-step problems, not verification failures on prod release runners).

question: In a Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem) upgrade using the private installer, the UI has been stuck on "Running migrations" for over an hour and the migration pod logs repeatedly say it's waiting on a Postgres advisory lock (the Postgres database is externally managed, not the in-cluster StatefulSet). What's the underlying cause, what Support-guided action resolved it, and what specific logs/artifacts should be collected before attempting a retry or rollback?
gold_answer: Cause: the migration job was blocked because another Postgres session (typically left over from a prior failed/aborted upgrade) was still holding the Postgres advisory lock, so the new migration pod kept waiting. Resolution: under Support guidance the customer's DBA identified the stale lock-holding session and terminated it, after which migrations proceeded and the upgrade completed. Collect before any retry/rollback: installer logs/output around when it entered "Running migrations" (including migration runner stdout/stderr) and start/stuck timestamps; versions (from/to platform version and installer version); Kubernetes artifacts for the migration job/pod (kubectl get jobs, get pods filtered for migrat/upgrade/install, describe the migration pod, logs from the migration pod/all containers with a tail or since window); recent namespace events (kubectl get events tail); and, for external Postgres, a DB-side lock/activity snapshot showing the advisory-lock holder/long-lived sessions (as available from the DBA).
answer_facts: The migration job was blocked because another Postgres session was still holding the Postgres advisory lock.
The lock holding session was typically left over from a prior failed or aborted upgrade.
Because the advisory lock was held, the new migration pod repeatedly waited on the Postgres advisory lock and migrations did not progress.
Under Support guidance, the customers DBA identified the stale session holding the advisory lock and terminated it.
After the stale lock holding session was terminated, migrations proceeded and the upgrade completed.

Before attempting a retry or rollback, collect the private installer logs and output from when the UI entered Running migrations, including migration runner stdout and stderr and start and stuck timestamps.
Before attempting a retry or rollback, collect the from and to platform versions and the installer version.

Before attempting a retry or rollback, collect Kubernetes artifacts for the migration job and pod, including kubectl get jobs, kubectl get pods filtered for migration or upgrade or install, and kubectl describe of the migration pod.
Before attempting a retry or rollback, collect logs from the migration pod for all containers, using an appropriate tail or since window.
Before attempting a retry or rollback, collect recent namespace events such as kubectl get events output.
Because Postgres is externally managed, collect a database side lock and activity snapshot showing the advisory lock holder and any long lived sessions.
The answer must not claim the Postgres database is the in-cluster StatefulSet (or advise kubectl exec into postgres-0) since the scenario specifies externally managed Postgres.
The answer must not attribute the stall to network/DNS/database reachability failures or storage/node DiskPressure/OOM; the key symptom is repeatedly waiting on a Postgres advisory lock.

The answer must not say the issue was resolved solely by restarting/deleting the migration pod or rerunning the installer without addressing the lock holder; the successful action was terminating the stale session holding the advisory lock.
The answer must not recommend manual schema downgrades as part of rollback; rollback requires confirming migration state and collecting diagnostics first.

question: In the March 2026 fix for Hosted API issues where SSE streams were truncated/partial behind corporate proxies (e.g., NGINX/Envoy idle timeouts), what resume identifier format did we choose for Last-Event-ID-style reconnects, and per our 2026 partial-result checkpointing/resume standard what are the default checkpoint emission cadence and checkpoint TTL?

question: In March 2026, what caused duplicate tool/webhook executions during *streaming* chat retries only when the idempotency key contained non-ASCII characters (e.g., emoji/diacritics) and the request went over HTTP/2 through the edge proxy-and what were the recommended workaround and the server-side fix (including what normalization we applied)?
gold_answer: It was caused by the idempotency key's raw header bytes changing across retries when non-ASCII/composed Unicode was used: different SDKs emitted different Unicode normalization (NFD vs NFC) and the HTTP/2 edge proxy sometimes percent-encoded/folded the header, so the server dedupe store saw two distinct keys and re-executed the tool/webhook. Workaround: avoid non-ASCII in idempotency keys or encode them (e.g., base64/hex) before sending. Server-side fix: canonicalize idempotency keys on ingestion/lookup by normalizing to UTF-8 NFC so NFD/NFC/edge-mutated variants map to the same key.
answer_facts: Duplicate tool/webhook executions happened because idempotency key header bytes changed across streaming chat retries when the key contained non-ASCII or composed Unicode characters.
Different SDKs emitted different Unicode normalization for the idempotency key (NFD vs NFC), producing different header byte sequences.
When the request went over HTTP/2 through the edge proxy, the proxy could percent-encode or fold the idempotency header, further changing its raw bytes.
Because the server deduplication store compared the raw idempotency key bytes, it treated the variants as distinct keys and re-executed the tool/webhook.
Recommended workaround was to avoid non-ASCII characters in idempotency keys or pre-encode the key (for example base64 or hex) before sending.
Server-side fix was to canonicalize idempotency keys on ingestion and lookup so NFD, NFC, and edge-mutated variants map to the same key.
The canonicalization applied was normalization of the idempotency key to UTF-8 NFC.

The answer must not attribute the duplicates to chunked transfer encoding or an idempotency header being split/folded across chunk boundaries, nor recommend the 'idempotency_in_body=true' workaround (that is a different incident).
The answer must not claim the duplicates were caused by reusing the same ephemeral idempotency key across a client-side batch (per-batch token reuse under burst streaming load).

The answer must not claim the issue was due to customers truncating an MD5-based idempotency token, proxy fixed-size header buffers, or proxy case-folding of hex strings, nor recommend sending the full 32-character MD5 as the workaround.

The answer must not describe the fix as a streaming timebox, idempotent cancel handling, or resume tokens/checkpoints for reconnect; those changes are unrelated to the non-ASCII idempotency key normalization bug.

question: In a Private deployment incident in early March 2026, a scheduled KMS envelope key rotation caused about an 18-minute gap where audit events weren't being indexed and some requests saw stale SSO attributes that broke RBAC reconciliation. What was the root cause, and what immediate mitigation/hotfix was applied?
gold_answer: Root cause was a KMS rotation race: the audit-forwarder tried to re-encrypt buffered/batched audit events during envelope key rotation, hit transient 403 plus a key-ID mapping change and (due to incorrect retry handling) dropped events, while the SSO assertion cache's invalidation (tied to the signing key fingerprint) lagged the signer-fingerprint index update, allowing stale attributes to be served and RBAC reconciliation to read stale state. Immediate mitigation/hotfix was to pause auto-rotation, run a manual backfill/re-ingest from the forwarder's local buffer, enable strict assertion-cache invalidation on fingerprint drift, and deploy a hotpatch to make the audit-forwarder treat key-ID drift/rotation errors as retryable and buffer events until re-encrypt succeeds.
answer_facts: The root cause was a KMS envelope key rotation race affecting the audit-forwarder and SSO assertion cache.
During envelope key rotation, the audit-forwarder attempted to re-encrypt buffered or batched audit events and encountered transient KMS 403 errors plus a key-ID mapping change.
Because of incorrect retry handling, the audit-forwarder dropped some audit events instead of retrying and buffering until re-encryption succeeded.
The SSO assertion cache invalidation was tied to the signing key fingerprint and lagged the signer-fingerprint index update, so stale SSO attributes were served.
Stale SSO attributes caused RBAC reconciliation to read stale state.
Immediate mitigation included pausing automatic KMS key rotation.
Immediate mitigation included manually backfilling or re-ingesting audit events from the audit-forwarders local buffer.
A hotfix enabled strict SSO assertion-cache invalidation on signing-key fingerprint drift.
A hotpatch changed the audit-forwarder to treat key-ID drift or rotation errors as retryable and to buffer events until re-encryption succeeds.

question: In the EU hosted API, streaming generate requests over HTTP/2 intermittently returned 502 only when the client included a ~12KB base64-encoded user-metadata header. What was the root cause in our proxy chain, and what server-side mitigation did we deploy to stop the 502s (include the header and cutoff/behavior)?
gold_answer: Root cause was the midproxy NGINX rejecting the request because the client's ~12KB base64 X-User-Meta header exceeded its default header/buffer limits ("client sent too large header"), causing NGINX to abort the upstream connection and the edge Envoy to surface it as an intermittent HTTP/2 streaming 502 ("disconnect/reset before headers"). Mitigation deployed was an edge header-filter that drops X-User-Meta when it is >4096 bytes (oversized header is removed so the request can proceed), which stopped the 502s.

The mitigation was an edge header filter that drops the X-User-Meta header when it is larger than 4096 bytes so the request can proceed.
Dropping oversized X-User-Meta headers at the edge stopped the 502s.

question: What is the procedure for an emergency rollback of a Serving Runtime release (Hosted and Dedicated)?
gold_answer: For an emergency rollback of a Serving Runtime release in Hosted and Dedicated, first pause/freeze any in-flight progressive rollout so it cannot auto-advance, then re-pin the runtime to a known-good prior runtime release tag, trigger a redeploy, and verify fleet health (e.g., 5xx rate, p95 TTFT, GPU OOMs) before closing the incident/change record. For Hosted, apply the rollback across regions in order (non-critical/canary first, then remaining canary, then all affected regions) and lock the tag to prevent re-advance; for Dedicated, identify impacted tenant clusters, pin and redeploy per cluster (coordinating customer comms as required), and keep the incident open until metrics are stable (≈30 minutes Hosted, ≈60 minutes Dedicated) while capturing CI/CD job links, affected regions/clusters, tags, timings, and verification evidence.
answer_facts: In an emergency rollback of a Serving Runtime release, first pause or freeze any in-flight progressive rollout so it cannot auto-advance.
Re-pin the serving runtime to a known-good prior runtime release tag.
Trigger a redeploy after re-pinning the runtime tag.
Verify fleet health after the rollback using metrics such as 5xx rate, p95 TTFT, and GPU OOMs.
Close the incident or change record only after verification is complete.
For Hosted, apply the rollback across regions in order: non-critical or canary first, then remaining canary, then all affected regions.
For Hosted, lock the runtime tag to prevent re-advance after rollback.
For Dedicated, identify the impacted tenant clusters.
For Dedicated, pin and redeploy per impacted tenant cluster.
For Dedicated, coordinate customer communications as required during the rollback.
Keep the incident open until metrics are stable, approximately 30 minutes for Hosted and approximately 60 minutes for Dedicated.
Capture incident evidence including CI/CD job links, affected regions or clusters, runtime tags, timings, and verification evidence.

question: What is Redwood Inference's end-to-end process for rotating secrets and credentials used by production services (including emergency rotation)?
gold_answer:
For planned rotation, the owning service team identifies the secret (Vault path) and **all consumers** via the Credential Dependencies inventory, confirms tier/cadence and access, then rotates using a repeatable pattern: preferably **dual-valid overlap** (generate new credential → write as a new Vault version without deleting old → deploy/update consumers to read latest and, if applicable, accept old+new → monitor auth/health for ≥30 minutes (≥60 for Tier 0) → disable/deprecate the old credential at the issuer → remove the old Vault version after the overlap window, default 7 days) or, if overlap isn't possible, a **cutover** (scheduled change window → change issuer-side credential → immediately update Vault → restart/redeploy all consumers → validate/rollback if needed). After any rotation, they complete the verification checklist (ensure all rollouts/reloads completed, monitor auth/dependency metrics, run critical canaries, confirm audit logs and that any break-glass access is revoked) and record the event in a change record.

For **emergency rotation** (suspected compromise/exposure/anomalies), an incident is opened and "emergency secret rotation" is declared (secret names/paths only), break-glass may be used via the approved workflow for **time-limited (60 min)** Tier 0/1 rotate access with full audit logging; the IC/Security/SRE prioritize and rotate **Tier 0 then Tier 1**, selecting dual-valid where possible or cutover otherwise, updating issuer + Vault and restarting/redeploying all consumers, pausing to reassess if impact worsens, recording exact timestamps, then revoking break-glass immediately and documenting rotated secrets/services plus any follow-up rotations (derived/cached tokens) in the incident timeline and post-incident summary for Security review.
answer_facts: For planned rotation, the owning service team identifies the secret (Vault path) and all consumers via the Credential Dependencies inventory.
For planned rotation, the owning service team confirms tier and cadence and access before rotating.

Preferred planned rotation method is dual-valid overlap: generate a new credential, write it as a new Vault version without deleting the old, deploy/update consumers to read the latest and if applicable accept both old and new, monitor authentication and service health for at least 30 minutes (at least 60 minutes for Tier 0), then disable or deprecate the old credential at the issuer, and remove the old Vault version after the overlap window (default 7 days).

If overlap is not possible, planned rotation uses a cutover: schedule a change window, change the credential at the issuer, immediately update Vault, restart or redeploy all consumers, and validate with rollback if needed.

After any rotation, the team completes a verification checklist that includes ensuring all rollouts/reloads completed, monitoring auth/dependency metrics, running critical canaries, confirming audit logs, and revoking any break-glass access.
After any rotation, the rotation event is recorded in a change record.
For emergency rotation (suspected compromise, exposure, or anomalies), an incident is opened and emergency secret rotation is declared using secret names/paths only.
During emergency rotation, break-glass access may be used via the approved workflow for time-limited (60 minute) Tier 0/1 rotate access with full audit logging.
During emergency rotation, IC/Security/SRE prioritize rotating Tier 0 then Tier 1.
During emergency rotation, they use dual-valid overlap where possible or cutover otherwise, update the issuer and Vault, and restart/redeploy all consumers.
During emergency rotation, they pause to reassess if impact worsens and record exact timestamps.

After emergency rotation, break-glass access is revoked immediately and the rotated secrets/services and any follow-up rotations (derived/cached tokens) are documented in the incident timeline and post-incident summary for Security review.

question: What is the complete SOP Redwood Inference must follow to enable "customer-managed audit log export" (to the customer's cloud storage) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments-including required prerequisites, security approvals, configuration steps, validation, customer communication, and ongoing operations/ownership?
gold_answer:
1) **Intake & scoping (CS/Support)**: Open an **internal-support Jira** (Support Request; prioritize P2 standard, P1 if audit deadline <14 days), and record **customer + org_id (Hosted) / dedicated_cluster_id (Dedicated) / Private cluster**, deployment mode, regions, sink (**S3/GCS/Azure Blob**), **bucket/container + prefix + destination region**, identity method (**assume-role/IRSA/workload identity/Azure federated identity**), desired audit-log categories (**platform audit logs only-no prompt/completion logging**), retention intent, and **ops/escalation contacts + target date**; CS confirms entitlement and that export is **regional by default** (cross-region only by exception).  

2) **Prerequisites & mandatory security controls (must be satisfied before enablement)**: Treat logs as **Customer-Sensitive Metadata** and ensure **no request bodies/model outputs/token strings**; require **TLS 1.2+ in transit** and **encryption at rest** on the customer destination (S3 SSE-S3/SSE-KMS, GCS managed keys/CMEK, Azure managed keys/CMK) and encrypt any Redwood staging with Redwood KMS; enforce **least privilege** using **assume-role/workload identity only** (no long-lived customer keys stored by Redwood) scoped to **write to a specific bucket/container prefix** (and list only if required for health checks); confirm **destination ownership/allowed account** and appropriate **egress controls/allowlisting** (private endpoints preferred; for Private, customer constrains egress); align/confirm **retention** (recommend 90 days; <30 days allowed but noted; >1 year requires CS contract confirmation); ensure "**logging about logging**" is enabled (events for enable/disable, destination/auth changes, failures/retries visible in internal dashboards). Attach required evidence in the Jira: **policy/trust/binding snippet, encryption confirmation, proof of ownership IDs, and (Private) network diagram/egress statement**.  

3) **Approvals (gates)**:  
- **Gate A (CS/Support)**: request completeness + scope validation (platform audit logs only, region alignment).  
- **Gate B (Security-required for all modes)**: Security reviews encryption/least privilege/region rules and leaves a Jira comment: **"Approved for enablement"** (name/date) before production changes.  
- **Gate C (Legal/Privacy-conditional)**: required for **cross-region export**, **additional PII fields beyond standard schema**, or **bespoke contract clauses**; cross-region additionally requires **Security approval + explicit customer acknowledgement**. If controls can't be met, file a **Security exception** and link it.  

4) **Configuration & enablement (route by deployment mode)**:  
- **Hosted (Eng Platform oncall via `rdw-admin`)**: `audit-export destination upsert` (sink/region/bucket-or-container/prefix/auth-mode/role or binding) → `audit-export enable` → ensure tenant flag **`audit_export_customer_sink_enabled=true`**.  
- **Dedicated (Eng Infra)**: Decide connector placement (**Redwood-managed default** vs **dedicated-placed** exception requiring **SRE review**). Default path: update per-tenant **Terraform `dedicated-config`** `audit_export` (enabled, sink, region, destination, prefix, auth_reference) and **plan/apply**. Exception path: deploy `audit-export-connector` into the dedicated cluster (Helm), use **short-lived identity**, and configure private endpoints/egress as required.  
- **Private (Customer operator with Private Deployments guidance; bundle v2.8.0+)**: Configure `redwood-private` Helm values `auditExport.enabled=true` with sink/region/destination/prefix/format and **workload identity** (AWS IRSA, GCP Workload Identity, Azure Workload Identity) plus any private-endpoint DNS/egress settings; redeploy and restart the connector.  

5) **Validation (Redwood + customer)**: Redwood verifies connector health (**delivery_success_rate**, **delivery_lag_seconds**, **write_errors_total**) and that the tenant/cluster consumer is active; customer (coordinated by CS) confirms objects land in the agreed prefix (e.g., Hosted `org=<ORG_ID>/region=<REGION>/dt=.../hr=.../*.jsonl.gz`, Dedicated `dedicated_cluster=<ID>/region=.../...`) within ~10 minutes, and both sides spot-check one file for required fields (e.g., `event_id`, `source_service`) and **absence of inference payload fields**; remind customer delivery is **at-least-once** and they must **deduplicate by `event_id`**.  

6) **Closeout documentation & customer communication (CS owns comms)**: Update the Jira with destination identifiers, auth reference, connector placement (Dedicated), approvals, enablement timestamp, and validation evidence; CS sends/updates the customer with confirmation, expected delivery semantics/latency, and reiterates the customer owns **storage cost, retention/lifecycle, SIEM ingestion, and destination access control**.  

7) **Ongoing operations/ownership & rollback**: Redwood owns connector operation/monitoring in **Hosted/Dedicated** (and provides troubleshooting support for **Private** if customer shares logs/metrics); paging/alerting follows policy (enterprise Dedicated/Private: page on sustained failure >30 min; Hosted: alert unless multi-tenant impact). Customers should alert on **no new objects >30 min** and sustained **4xx/5xx** from storage and must notify Redwood before rotating identity/policies. If issues occur (403/trust drift, throttling, DNS/TLS/private endpoint failures), coordinate Eng + Security/CS; to stop export, **disable** (Hosted `rdw-admin audit-export disable`; Dedicated set `audit_export.enabled=false` in Terraform; Private `auditExport.enabled=false` and redeploy). If a **data-boundary/security concern** is suspected, **disable immediately** and escalate per incident process.
answer_facts: Enablement starts with CS/Support opening an internal-support Jira ticket and recording customer identity plus org_id for Hosted, dedicated_cluster_id for Dedicated, or the Private cluster identifier.

The intake ticket must capture deployment mode, regions, destination sink type (S3, GCS, or Azure Blob), bucket or container name, prefix, destination region, identity method (assume-role, IRSA, workload identity, or Azure federated identity), desired audit-log categories, retention intent, ops escalation contacts, and target date.
CS must confirm entitlement and that export is regional by default, with cross-region export only by exception.
Before enablement, Redwood must treat audit logs as Customer-Sensitive Metadata and ensure logs do not include request bodies, model outputs, or token strings.

Before enablement, data in transit must use TLS 1.2 or higher and the customer destination must have encryption at rest enabled (for example SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS on S3, managed keys or CMEK on GCS, managed keys or CMK on Azure).
Any Redwood staging involved in export must be encrypted using Redwood KMS.
Authentication must use least privilege via assume-role or workload identity only, with no long-lived customer keys stored by Redwood.
The destination permissions must be scoped to writing to a specific bucket or container prefix, with list permission only if required for health checks.
Redwood must confirm destination ownership or allowed account and required egress controls or allowlisting (private endpoints preferred; for Private deployments the customer constrains egress).
Retention expectations must be aligned during prerequisites (recommend 90 days; more than 1 year requires CS contract confirmation).
Redwood must ensure logging about logging is enabled so enable or disable actions, destination or auth changes, and failures or retries are visible in internal dashboards.

The Jira must include evidence such as the destination policy or trust or binding snippet, encryption confirmation, proof of ownership identifiers, and for Private deployments a network diagram or egress statement.
Gate A approval is CS/Support confirming request completeness and scope validation (platform audit logs only and region alignment).
Gate B approval is Security review for all deployment modes, and Security must comment Approved for enablement with name and date before production changes.

Gate C approval from Legal or Privacy is required when export is cross-region, when adding PII fields beyond the standard schema, or when bespoke contract clauses apply, and cross-region export also requires explicit customer acknowledgement.
If required controls cannot be met, a Security exception must be filed and linked in the Jira.

Hosted enablement is performed by Engineering Platform oncall using rdw-admin to upsert the audit-export destination, enable audit export, and ensure the tenant flag audit_export_customer_sink_enabled is set to true.

Dedicated enablement is performed by Engineering Infra either by updating per-tenant Terraform dedicated-config audit_export settings and applying, or by deploying an audit-export connector into the dedicated cluster as an exception that requires SRE review.

Private enablement is performed by the customer operator using Private deployment guidance (bundle v2.8.0 or later) by setting Helm values auditExport.enabled=true with sink, region, destination, prefix, format, and workload identity, then redeploying and restarting the connector.
Validation requires Redwood to verify connector health metrics including delivery_success_rate, delivery_lag_seconds, and write_errors_total and confirm the tenant or cluster consumer is active.
Validation requires the customer to confirm objects land in the agreed prefix within about 10 minutes and to spot-check a file for required fields and for absence of inference payload fields.
Customers must be told delivery is at-least-once and they must deduplicate using event_id.
Closeout requires updating the Jira with destination identifiers, auth reference, connector placement for Dedicated, approvals, enablement timestamp, and validation evidence.

Customer communication is owned by CS and must confirm enablement, expected delivery latency and semantics, and that the customer owns storage cost, retention and lifecycle, SIEM ingestion, and destination access control.
Ongoing operations ownership is Redwood monitoring and operating the connector for Hosted and Dedicated, and providing troubleshooting support for Private when the customer shares logs or metrics.
Customers should alert on no new objects for more than 30 minutes and sustained 4xx or 5xx from storage, and must notify Redwood before rotating identity or policies.
Rollback or stop procedures are: Hosted run rdw-admin audit-export disable, Dedicated set audit_export.enabled=false in Terraform, and Private set auditExport.enabled=false and redeploy.
If a data-boundary or security concern is suspected, export must be disabled immediately and escalated per the incident process.

question: For an enterprise Redwood Private deployment upgrade, what is the complete go/no-go gate (required validations, required approvals, and required customer communications) mandated by Redwood's internal standards?
gold_answer:
Redwood's internal go/no-go gate for an enterprise Redwood Private production deployment upgrade requires that, at least 24h before the maintenance window, there is a change record created from the Private Upgrade Change Request template with a single named Change Owner and confirmed customer-side Change Approver and Operator contacts who will be present. The change record must document current and target versions (installer, operator/controller, runtime images, charts), the exact supported upgrade path (default N-1 to N; no unsupported major skips), and any model/runtime compatibility notes for pinned models. Rollback readiness must be demonstrated with recent backups (control-plane config state such as values/helm releases/operator CRs, any Redwood-managed PVs if present, and documentation of customer external dependencies like DB/SSO/KMS), locally available rollback artifacts (previous installer bundle, prior image tags, prior values files), a written rollback plan with commands and a time-to-rollback/time-to-restore estimate, and an explicit rollback decision point in the window plan. Preflight validations must be run and attached as evidence (standard preflight output) covering cluster capacity and node readiness, storage class/PV health, DNS and egress reachability for image pulls (or offline bundle availability where applicable), TLS/certificate status, and required Kubernetes API versions present (no deprecated APIs that will break charts). Observability readiness must be confirmed: Redwood has dashboard/alert visibility during the window (temporary access if needed) and the watch signals are available at minimum for control plane API error rate, inference 5xx, P95 latency on primary routes, and GPU utilization plus OOM/restart rates.

At window start, the Change Owner may record GO in the change record only after all required sign-offs are linked and complete: Change Management approvals per risk level (Private production upgrades are at least High), SRE SLO/error budget impact review is completed with evidence (30-day error budget remaining and explicit risk acceptance if below 25%, capacity/headroom check, monitoring watch list and alert routing to active on-call, rollback feasibility and the decision point, and confirmation of no overlapping high-risk changes), Security and Compliance sign-off is completed if triggered (auth/SSO/JWT/mTLS, RBAC/privileged accounts, TLS/cert management/trust stores, audit logging pipeline/retention/integrity, new third-party images/dependencies with digests and scan summary, or network boundary changes), and Customer Success acknowledges customer readiness and the comms plan. Final technical readiness at T=0 must be reconfirmed: preflight has no red blockers (and any yellow warnings have an owner and mitigation), there are no open Redwood-side P0/P1 incidents that would reduce ability to respond or rollback, and any change-freeze exception is approved and linked.

Customer communications must also be ready at GO time per the Planned Maintenance Comms SOP: a Customer Success-approved maintenance notice has been sent via the agreed channel with the required lead time (minimum 48h for enterprise; longer lead times may apply per risk tier), including the window in the customers timezone, expected impact, rollback posture/thresholds, required customer participation, and escalation contacts; internal heads-up has been posted at least 48h prior in #eng-oncall and #customer-success with customer name, window, change owner, link to the change record, and whether security sign-off is required (and per Private release gating, internal comms also go to #eng-releases); the window-start message is prepared to confirm backout plan and next update time; progress updates are planned at a 30-minute cadence with immediate updates for rollback or risk escalation; and an all-clear message is drafted for after the post-upgrade verification window. For Private upgrades, customer-specific maintenance is communicated directly to the customer and not posted to the public status page unless contractually required.
